notes.body: An old province covering much of present-day Chiba and part of Ibaraki. 下 means “lower,” while 総 belongs to the inherited provincial name Fusa; 下総 is therefore a fixed place-name rather than a freely composed phrase meaning “lower total.” 国 adds “province” or “country.”

葛飾 is an inherited place-name. 葛 means “kudzu vine” and 飾 means “to adorn,” but those two meanings do not transparently produce the geographical name. 郡 means an administrative district; the translation retains the older reading こおり rather than modern ぐん.

真間 is the old place-name Mamma, in present-day Ichikawa, and was already a poetic place in the Man’yōshū. 真 means “true” and 間 “interval,” but the name is not understood by adding those glosses. 里 is a village or rural settlement.

A masculine personal name. 勝 contributes the idea of victory or excellence; 四郎 is a traditional male name element, literally “fourth son.” Together they form a personal name, not a description of the character.
In 勝四郎という男, 男 is the general noun “man.” The whole expression means “a man called Katsushirō.”
The past form of ある, “to exist” or “to be.” In the opening of a story, 男があった is naturally understood as “there was a man” or “there lived a man,” not as possession.

祖 means an ancestor or forebear and 父 means father; together 祖父 is “grandfather.” 代 means a generation or period. Thus 祖父の代 is “the grandfather’s generation,” the point from which the family’s residence is measured.
The adverbial form of 久しい, “long-standing” or “of long duration.” It tells us that the family’s rootedness in the village was not recent.
The continuative form of 住む, “to live” or “to dwell.” It joins the family’s long residence to the possessions and prosperity that follow.
田 is a wet field, especially a rice paddy; 畑 is a dry or upland field. Together 田畑 gathers the principal kinds of cultivated land into one expression: the family’s farmland as a whole.
An ordinary modern word for abundance. Here it modifies the amount of land the family possessed: 田畑もたくさん.
The て-form of 持つ, “to hold” or “to possess.” Here it connects possession to the next clause: they had many fields and lived prosperously.
Here 家 is broader than the physical building. It means the household and its material condition—the family as an established domestic unit.
豊か describes abundance, amplitude, or material ease. 家も豊かに暮らしていた means that the household lived in prosperity.
暮らす means “to live” or “to pass one’s days.” 暮らしていた combines its て-form with the past progressive/state form いた, presenting the family’s prosperity as an established condition extending through time.
Something present from birth rather than acquired later. The source writes 生れつき; contemporary orthography more often writes 生まれつき.

無 means “without.” 頓着 is the established word for concern, heed, or attention; its meaning is not usefully recovered by mechanically adding 頓 and 着. At the larger level, however, 無 + 頓着 is transparent: “without concern,” hence unconcerned or heedless.
A relaxed freedom from worry. In another setting it can be pleasant, but here it shades into carelessness: Katsushirō’s ease prevents him from attending to the work that sustains the household.
性 is one’s nature or character; 質 is the underlying substance or quality of a thing. Together 性質 is the quality constituting a person’s character—his disposition.
農 means agriculture or cultivation; 業 means work, occupation, or sustained activity. Together 農業 is agriculture as an occupation: farming.
Something that demands irritating effort, attention, or complication. Katsushirō does not merely find farming difficult; he experiences its necessary demands as an encumbrance.
いや means unpleasant or unwelcome. The suffix がる expresses showing or feeling that state, and the past form がった presents his settled aversion: he treated farming as something he did not want to do.
Marks the endpoint reached after a process. It gathers the earlier causes—carelessness and aversion to work—into their eventual result.

notes.term: 貧乏になってしまった
notes.reading: びんぼうになってしまった
notes.body: 貧 means poverty or impoverishment and 乏 means lack or scarcity; together 貧乏 is poverty, and as a な-adjective it means “poor.” なる gives “to become,” while てしまった presents the change as completed and, here, regrettable: he ended up poor.
